Unlike conventional portals that charge listing fees, Find My Move operates on an advertising-supported model. The founders suggest this approach could prove particularly valuable for agents facing financial pressures in the current market. Vine commented: "Many agents felt trapped paying increasingly large sums simply to showcase their properties. We wanted to create a more accessible route to market."

The platform's early adoption figures appear to support this premise, with Moss noting: "The engagement levels we're seeing demonstrate a clear appetite for alternative property solutions." Industry observers might view this development as part of a broader trend towards diversifying property marketing channels.

For homeowners and landlords considering property portals, this new entrant could represent an additional avenue to explore when marketing a home. With the platform currently free to use and showing strong early growth, property professionals might wish to monitor its evolution in the coming months as the founders work to expand their user base and commercial partnerships.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

New Free Property Portal Gains Traction Among UK Agents

A fresh property listing platform has emerged in the UK market, offering estate and letting agents a cost-free alternative to traditional portals. Find My Move, founded by industry professionals Mark Vine and Chris Moss, has rapidly attracted over 9,000 subscribers since its launch. The portal currently showcases more than 435,000 homes for sale and 58,000 rental properties sourced from 6,600 agencies nationwide.

The founders report steady growth, with approximately 3,000 new users joining each month and over 200,000 visitors in the last quarter. Interestingly, more than half of registered users are actively engaged in property searches. The platform's development stemmed from Vine's firsthand experience of rising advertising costs in the lettings sector.

Alternative Approach to Property Advertising

Unlike conventional portals that charge listing fees, Find My Move operates on an advertising-supported model. The founders suggest this approach could prove particularly valuable for agents facing financial pressures in the current market. Vine commented: "Many agents felt trapped paying increasingly large sums simply to showcase their properties. We wanted to create a more accessible route to market."

The platform's early adoption figures appear to support this premise, with Moss noting: "The engagement levels we're seeing demonstrate a clear appetite for alternative property solutions." Industry observers might view this development as part of a broader trend towards diversifying property marketing channels.

For homeowners and landlords considering property portals, this new entrant could represent an additional avenue to explore when marketing a home. With the platform currently free to use and showing strong early growth, property professionals might wish to monitor its evolution in the coming months as the founders work to expand their user base and commercial partnerships.

item.title: Redundancy talks underway – but estate agency eyes ‘key hires’
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Knight Frank has announced new appointments to its affiliate broker programme whilst simultaneously entering redundancy consultations affecting parts of its UK workforce. The estate agency confirmed an undisclosed number of roles are under review, with industry sources suggesting between 90 and 100 employees could be impacted.

Affiliate broker expansion continues

The company is pressing ahead with its innovative affiliate model, which enables self-employed brokers to operate independently under the Knight Frank brand. These brokers handle client relationships and business development whilst receiving operational and marketing support from the firm. The structure also allows them to earn referral fees by introducing clients to Knight Frank's wider property services.

Three experienced agents - Simon Boulton, Mark Lee and Mark Ruffell - have been named as the first affiliate brokers. All three previously held leadership roles within Knight Frank's London operations. The company reports they've already completed several high-value transactions, including:

  • A £6.25m penthouse sale in Butler's Wharf
  • A £6m apartment transaction in Casson Square

London growth strategy

This initiative forms part of Knight Frank's ambitious plan to establish presence across 36 London locations. The firm has specifically identified Putney, Totteridge and Kingston as target markets for expansion. Rory Penn, Head of London Residential Sales at Knight Frank, commented: "This marks a pivotal moment for our London residential business. Our first affiliate brokers bring exceptional track records in prime London markets."

Industry observers note this dual approach of workforce restructuring alongside strategic hiring reflects the challenging conditions in the UK property market. The affiliate model potentially offers Knight Frank greater flexibility to scale operations up or down according to market demand.

As the redundancy consultation progresses, the company's focus appears firmly fixed on this new brokerage model as a key component of its future growth strategy in London's competitive property landscape.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Knight Frank has announced new appointments to its affiliate broker programme whilst simultaneously entering redundancy consultations affecting parts of its UK workforce. The estate agency confirmed an undisclosed number of roles are under review, with industry sources suggesting between 90 and 100 employees could be impacted.

Affiliate broker expansion continues

The company is pressing ahead with its innovative affiliate model, which enables self-employed brokers to operate independently under the Knight Frank brand. These brokers handle client relationships and business development whilst receiving operational and marketing support from the firm. The structure also allows them to earn referral fees by introducing clients to Knight Frank's wider property services.

Three experienced agents - Simon Boulton, Mark Lee and Mark Ruffell - have been named as the first affiliate brokers. All three previously held leadership roles within Knight Frank's London operations. The company reports they've already completed several high-value transactions, including:

  • A £6.25m penthouse sale in Butler's Wharf
  • A £6m apartment transaction in Casson Square

London growth strategy

This initiative forms part of Knight Frank's ambitious plan to establish presence across 36 London locations. The firm has specifically identified Putney, Totteridge and Kingston as target markets for expansion. Rory Penn, Head of London Residential Sales at Knight Frank, commented: "This marks a pivotal moment for our London residential business. Our first affiliate brokers bring exceptional track records in prime London markets."

Industry observers note this dual approach of workforce restructuring alongside strategic hiring reflects the challenging conditions in the UK property market. The affiliate model potentially offers Knight Frank greater flexibility to scale operations up or down according to market demand.

As the redundancy consultation progresses, the company's focus appears firmly fixed on this new brokerage model as a key component of its future growth strategy in London's competitive property landscape.

item.title: Greater education needed? Student landlords left confused by Renters’ Rights Act
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The introduction of the Renters’ Rights Act has brought significant changes to the UK’s rental market, but student landlords are reportedly struggling to navigate its implications. Many are turning to social media and online forums for guidance, highlighting a lack of clarity around how the legislation applies to student-specific tenancies. This confusion could lead to delays in regaining possession, void periods, and lost rental income, according to student lettings agency loc8me.

Challenges for Student Landlords

Unlike traditional private rentals, the student housing market operates on a unique academic cycle, with tenancies typically aligned to the academic year. Fern Tebbutt, director of operations at loc8me, notes that parts of the Renters’ Rights Act, while logical for standard private landlords, don’t necessarily address the specific challenges faced by student landlords. This has left many feeling excluded from the broader conversation around the legislation.

Areas of particular confusion include Ground 4A, a new possession ground designed for certain student houses in multiple occupation (HMOs). Some landlords mistakenly believe this applies to all student properties, when in fact one and two-bedroom homes are excluded. Errors in serving notice or preparing tenancy documentation could prevent landlords from relying on this ground when seeking possession ahead of the next academic year.

Misconceptions Around Section 21

Another common misunderstanding is the abolition of Section 21, which has led some landlords to believe they can no longer regain possession of their properties. Whilst the process has changed, loc8me emphasises that possession remains possible through statutory grounds, provided the correct procedures are followed. This includes adhering to notice periods and ensuring documentation is accurate.

There are also concerns that the reforms could lead to increased numbers of students ending tenancies mid-academic year. However, whilst tenants now have greater flexibility, they must still follow the legal process and provide the required notice to terminate a tenancy. This suggests that the student rental market’s cyclical nature is likely to remain intact.

Geographical Hotspots of Confusion

According to loc8me, self-managing landlords in cities with large student populations, such as Nottingham, Leeds, Manchester, Bristol, and Liverpool, are among the most affected by the uncertainty. These areas are also adapting to wider regulatory changes, adding to the complexity. The agency warns that relying on outdated guidance or informal online commentary could result in costly mistakes.

With demand for student accommodation continuing to outstrip supply in many university towns, it’s crucial for landlords to stay informed and seek professional advice where necessary. Clearer guidance tailored to the student rental market could help alleviate some of the confusion and ensure smoother management of student properties.

As the Renters’ Rights Act continues to reshape the rental landscape, student landlords may wish to familiarise themselves with the specific provisions affecting their sector. Doing so could help avoid unnecessary complications and ensure compliance with the new regulations.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The introduction of the Renters’ Rights Act has brought significant changes to the UK’s rental market, but student landlords are reportedly struggling to navigate its implications. Many are turning to social media and online forums for guidance, highlighting a lack of clarity around how the legislation applies to student-specific tenancies. This confusion could lead to delays in regaining possession, void periods, and lost rental income, according to student lettings agency loc8me.

Challenges for Student Landlords

Unlike traditional private rentals, the student housing market operates on a unique academic cycle, with tenancies typically aligned to the academic year. Fern Tebbutt, director of operations at loc8me, notes that parts of the Renters’ Rights Act, while logical for standard private landlords, don’t necessarily address the specific challenges faced by student landlords. This has left many feeling excluded from the broader conversation around the legislation.

Areas of particular confusion include Ground 4A, a new possession ground designed for certain student houses in multiple occupation (HMOs). Some landlords mistakenly believe this applies to all student properties, when in fact one and two-bedroom homes are excluded. Errors in serving notice or preparing tenancy documentation could prevent landlords from relying on this ground when seeking possession ahead of the next academic year.

Misconceptions Around Section 21

Another common misunderstanding is the abolition of Section 21, which has led some landlords to believe they can no longer regain possession of their properties. Whilst the process has changed, loc8me emphasises that possession remains possible through statutory grounds, provided the correct procedures are followed. This includes adhering to notice periods and ensuring documentation is accurate.

There are also concerns that the reforms could lead to increased numbers of students ending tenancies mid-academic year. However, whilst tenants now have greater flexibility, they must still follow the legal process and provide the required notice to terminate a tenancy. This suggests that the student rental market’s cyclical nature is likely to remain intact.

Geographical Hotspots of Confusion

According to loc8me, self-managing landlords in cities with large student populations, such as Nottingham, Leeds, Manchester, Bristol, and Liverpool, are among the most affected by the uncertainty. These areas are also adapting to wider regulatory changes, adding to the complexity. The agency warns that relying on outdated guidance or informal online commentary could result in costly mistakes.

With demand for student accommodation continuing to outstrip supply in many university towns, it’s crucial for landlords to stay informed and seek professional advice where necessary. Clearer guidance tailored to the student rental market could help alleviate some of the confusion and ensure smoother management of student properties.

As the Renters’ Rights Act continues to reshape the rental landscape, student landlords may wish to familiarise themselves with the specific provisions affecting their sector. Doing so could help avoid unnecessary complications and ensure compliance with the new regulations.
